A lock-free ring buffer manages wraparound by treating the underlying storage as a circular array where indices are incremented modulo the buffer size, ensuring that logical positions map correctly to physical memory slots without explicit boundary checks during normal operation. To maintain data integrity without locks, the implementation relies on atomic compare-and-swap operations and carefully ordered memory barriers, which enforce a happens-before relationship between producer writes and consumer reads to prevent race conditions and ensure visibility of data across CPU cores. Backpressure is typically handled by having the producer check if the buffer is full before attempting an insertion, often returning an error code or blocking briefly, rather than dynamically resizing the buffer,
